pattern: Sorting both strings and comparing element-wise reveals whether one dominates the other at every position.
hints: A permutation that breaks another can always be found if and only if the sorted version of one string dominates the sorted version of the other — mismatched permutations can only hurt.
After sorting both strings, check two conditions: does every character of s1 beat or tie s2? Or does every character of s2 beat or tie s1?
If either dominance direction holds across all positions simultaneously, the answer is true; a single counter-example in both directions means false.

edge_inputs.description: s1 all 'a', s2 all 'z' — s2 dominates
interleaved dominance — neither side wins uniformly
s2 can break s1 (not s1 breaking s2) — should still return true
all same character — all ties count as break
mixed: s1 breaks s2 but not vice-versa

stress.description: 10000-char strings where one dominates the other by construction
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = size
    s1 = ''.join(rng.choices(string.ascii_lowercase[:13], k=n))
    s2 = ''.join(rng.choices(string.ascii_lowercase[13:], k=n))
    return [s1, s2]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def checkIfCanBreak(self, s1, s2):
        a, b = sorted(s1), sorted(s2)
        n = len(a)
        s1_breaks = all(a[i] >= b[i] for i in range(n))
        s2_breaks = all(b[i] >= a[i] for i in range(n))
        return s1_breaks or s2_breaks
